mappings.rationaleAI: The Silk Road's maritime routes connecting Southeast Asia with broader trade networks relate to South and East Asian influences on Southeast Asian civilizations (A) and the importance of maritime trade with China for Srivijaya (C.2).

Thailand's history from early Tai confederacies, Sukhothai, and Ayutthaya periods directly addresses the Tai people and kingdom of Siam (B.2), including origins (B.2.a), Sukhothai establishment (B.2.b), and Ayutthaya state (B.2.c).

The post-classical period's coverage of Southeast Asian kingdoms provides broad context for Southeast Asian civilizations' character (A), mainland Southeast Asia (B), and the Indonesian archipelago (C).
Asian history's coverage of Southeast Asian kingdoms including Khmer and Vietnam provides context across the section's major topics (A, B, C).
Mongol destruction of Pagan in 1287 directly relates to the city kingdom of Pagan's destruction by the Mongols (B.1.c).
Mongol military campaigns reaching Southeast Asia relate to the destruction of Pagan (B.1.c) and the Singhasari empire's interaction with Mongol expansion (C.4).
Mahayana Buddhism including Vietnamese and Southeast Asian forms relates to the influence of Buddhism on Srivijaya's maritime kingdom (C.2) and religious development in Central Java (C.3).
Myanmar's history from prehistoric city-states through Pagan, Taungoo kingdoms, and Theravada Buddhism directly addresses Myanmar from the Anyathian culture through reunification (B.1).
Portuguese conquest of Malacca (1511) and early European intrusions into Southeast Asia directly address the Portuguese presence in Malaya (B.5.b).
Spanish maritime exploration and the Manila galleon trade provide context for early European intrusions into Southeast Asian trade networks (B.5.b).
Malaysia's history spanning Malay sultanates and the rise of Malacca directly addresses Malaya to the 16th century, including the sultanate of Malacca (B.5.a).
Singapore's origins as the maritime emporium Temasek on the Malay Peninsula relate to the formation of Malaya and coastal trade (B.5).
Jakarta's history from the Sunda Kingdom port of Sunda Kelapa on Java relates to the settlement of the Indonesian Archipelago and Javanese kingdoms (C).
The early modern period's coverage of European colonization of Asia relates to early European intrusions and the Portuguese conquest of Malacca (B.5.b).
Imperialism and European expansion provide context for the Portuguese and Spanish presence in Southeast Asia (B.5.b).

Ancient history's coverage of Southeast Asia and Oceania (Austronesia) provides context for the character of Southeast Asian civilizations (A) and early origins of Myanmar (B.1.a), Cambodia (B.3.a), and Vietnam (B.4.a).
